availability.p: Published under a Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)

editorialDecl.p: Encoding of this text has followed the recommendations of the LombardPress 1.0.0 guidelines for a diplomatic edition.

change.p: Created file for the first time.

div.head: Chapter 12 [Bnf 12926 Transcription]
div.p: Chapter 12.

¶ All that has been said above, and the ten arguments previously set forth, are effectively confirmed by the praiseworthy constitution and holy custom of the Church. For the custom of the Church is to be imitated and emulated in all things, according to Thomas, Secunda Secundae, q. 10, a. 12. c. And Quodlibet 2, a. 7, in the body. For whatever sacred men have written holds authority only from the Church; therefore the customs of the Church are to be praised and followed and set before the teaching of Jerome and Augustine, and of any other sacred doctor. So teaches Saint Thomas.

This being granted, never from the ascension of Christ to the heavenly realm did the Church begin with the punishment of the sins by which pagans offend God, in order to plant evangelical truth; rather, by gentleness, acts of service, meekness, and charity she drew them to the faith, offering them in the name of Christ the remission of all sins (as will be shown below, ch. 39 and the following chapters, more clearly than light itself).

For the Church, illuminated by the Holy Spirit, understands that Christ reserved to himself the judgment of those crimes, and did not commit it to her, and that he willed his Church to lead unbelievers to the truth of the faith by gentleness and charity, not by force or arms. And therefore among the decrees of the pontiffs we read: 45 dist. c. Those who with sincere intention desire to lead those outside the Christian religion to the right faith ought to strive by means of persuasion, not harshness, lest those whose minds sound reasoning might have called back from error be driven further away by hostility. For whoever acts otherwise and under this pretext wishes to remove them from the accustomed practice of their rite, is shown to be pursuing his own interests there rather than God's.

And further on: For what profit is there when, even if they have been forbidden against long-standing custom, it avails nothing for their conversion to the faith? Or how do we lay down rules for them if by this we cannot win them over? For the effort must be made that, rather, provoked by reason and gentleness, they should wish to follow us, not flee from us, and so forth.

The same view is held by Gregory in Book 11 of the Letters, Letter 15 and Letter 34, where he speaks in these words: Those who are at variance with the Christian religion must be gathered into the unity of the faith by gentleness, kindness, admonishment, and persuasion, lest those whom the sweetness of preaching and the fear of the coming judgment's dread might have invited to belief should be driven away by threats and terrors. It is therefore fitting that they come together willingly to hear the word of God from you, rather than shrinking from a harshness that is extended beyond all measure. Thus Gregory.

By these words Gregory sufficiently signifies what we have at length urged above, and what will be seen more fully below. What inconveniences, evils, and harms would follow if the Church were to begin the preaching of the Gospel from the punishment of sins. For war and force, injuries, slaughters, and plundering turn pagans away from religion — those who, admonished by gentleness and reason, might perhaps be healed by that which is the charity of God toward humankind.

The same song is sung by Saint Cyril in Book 6, Against Julian, who speaks thus: Since the Maker of all things willed man to be his own master and to be governed in his deeds by his own will, for this reason it seemed very rightly good to Christ, the Savior of all, that men should be freed from shameful deeds by persuasion rather than by the compulsion of necessity, so that they might choose what is better and be made better by those means by which it is most plausible they would be so. For if, adorned with royal glory and holding invincible power, he had commanded men to believe in him, faith would no longer have been the fruit of knowledge, but rather of necessary and unavoidable commands, and he might perhaps have been like one who is honored with the glory of divinity unworthily, according to human opinions. But because he comported himself as one of us, with modest and humble bearing, and abstained from human glory, partly by his most wise teachings, partly by his ineffable divine miracles, he leads the willing — and those who truly know — to better things, and so forth.

See how far removed from the decrees of the Fathers and from the teaching of Christ is the desire to bring pagans to the faith by the terror of arms, military force, and empty arrogance. But whoever acts otherwise, or teaches that this should be done, shows that both parties are intent upon plunder, as Gregory in the place cited above shows: they are shown to be pursuing their own interests there rather than God's.

Finally, that Sepúlveda has gone wholly astray here, and that those who abuse the authority of that otherwise most learned man for the confirmation of an impious cause are instruments of the devil, the enemy of human salvation, of the glory of Christ, and of the propagation of the Gospel — this was declared by the Roman Pontiff Paul III, Vicar of Christ, in the year 1537, by means of a bull he issued, which confirms and renews the ancient custom of the Church in the preaching of the Gospel and the subjugation of pagans to the true religion. It has pleased us to append here the text of the bull. ¶ The sublime God so loved the human race that he created man of such a nature that he might not only share in the good, as other creatures do, but might also attain the highest good — the inaccessible good — and see it face to face.

And further on: Thereupon, the Truth itself, which can neither deceive nor be deceived, is known to have said, when it appointed preachers of the faith to the office of preaching: "Going, teach all nations" — it said "all," without any distinction, since all are capable of receiving the discipline of the faith. Seeing and envying this, that rival of the human race who always opposes all good things lest they be saved, devised a hitherto unheard-of means of hindering the word of God from being preached to the nations so that they might be saved, and stirred up certain of his satellites who, desiring to satisfy their own greed, presume everywhere to assert that the western and southern Indians, and other peoples who have in these times come to our notice, should be reduced to our service like brute animals, on the pretext that they are without the Catholic faith; and they reduce them to slavery, pressing them with such afflictions as those by which scarcely brute animals serving them would be pressed.

We, therefore, who, though unworthy, exercise on earth the functions of the same our Lord, and seek with all our effort to bring to his sheepfold those sheep of his flock that are outside it, and that have been entrusted to us — taking note that the Indians themselves, as true human beings, are not only capable of the Christian faith but, as has come to our attention, eagerly hasten toward that faith — and wishing to provide suitable remedies for these matters, we determine and declare by apostolic authority, by these presents, that the aforesaid Indians and all other peoples that shall hereafter come to the knowledge of Christians, even though they be outside the faith of Christ, are nonetheless not to be deprived or to be deprived of their liberty or dominion over their things; rather, they may freely and lawfully use, possess, and enjoy such liberty and dominion; nor ought they to be reduced to slavery; and whatever has been done contrary to this is void and of no force or effect. And we decree and declare by the said apostolic authority that the said Indians and all other peoples are to be invited to the said faith of Christ by the preaching of the word of God and the example of a good life, notwithstanding the foregoing and whatever else may be to the contrary. Given at Rome, and so forth. These are its formal words.

The designated executor and commissioner of this bull by apostolic authority was the Archbishop of Toledo, with the fullest power, speaking as follows. ¶ Our beloved son, greeting and apostolic blessing. Exercising with diligent care the pastoral office toward the sheep entrusted to us from on high, as we are afflicted by their perdition, so we rejoice in their advancement. And we not only praise their good works, but we extend more widely the concerns of our apostolic consideration so that they may enjoy the outcomes they have prayed for. Indeed it has come to our hearing that our most dear son in Christ, Charles, ever august Emperor of the Romans, who is also King of Castile and León, has by public edict forbidden all his subjects, in order to restrain those who, burning with greed, bear an inhuman disposition toward humankind, from presuming to reduce the western or southern Indians to slavery or to deprive them of their goods.

p.quote: We therefore, taking note that the Indians themselves, even though they exist outside the bosom of the Church, are nonetheless not deprived nor to be deprived of their liberty or dominion over their things — and since they are human beings and therefore capable of faith and salvation, they are not to be destroyed by servitude but are to be invited to life by preachings and examples — and therefore also desiring to restrain for our part the wicked audacity of such impious men, and to provide lest, embittered by injuries and harms, they become more hardened against embracing the faith of Christ, we have special confidence in the Lord in your circumspection, rectitude, prudence, piety, and experience in these and other matters.

div.p: By these presents we commit and command that by yourself or through one or more others you stand as a shield of effective defense for all the aforesaid Indians in the matters set forth above, and that you strictly forbid, under pain of excommunication already incurred by the act itself if they do otherwise — from which they cannot be absolved except by us or by the Roman Pontiff who is in office at the time, except for those in the moment of death, and with prior satisfaction — forbid all and singular, of whatever dignity, estate, condition, rank, and excellence they may be, from in any way presuming to reduce the aforesaid Indians to slavery or to despoil them of their goods in any manner; and that you proceed against those who do not comply to the declaration of the incurrence of this excommunication and to further measures, and that in the aforesaid matters and those related you establish, order, and dispose what is necessary or in any way fitting, as shall seem expedient to your prudence, probity, and religious sense. In which matters we grant you full power, and let nothing contrary to it stand, whatever it may be. Given at Rome, and so forth. These are its formal words.

¶ Do you see, reader, that the Roman Pontiff expressly asserts and teaches that all those things which these enemies of the propagation of the Gospel affirm are false? First, he teaches that the method used by these men was the invention of the devil, to impede the salvation of humankind and the propagation of the true religion. And in truth what these men have done must be called by that name — portraying the Indians as similar to wild and brute animals so as to make use of their labor, as though they were beasts of burden. And would that they had treated them no less savagely and conveniently than beasts of burden.

Second, the Roman Pontiff asserts that the Indians, inasmuch as they are human beings, are capable of blessedness and of the means by which one arrives at it, namely Christian teaching; and so he calls them sheep of the flock of Christ committed to his pastoral care, so that he may lead them to the sheepfold of Christ, outside of which they have hitherto been.

¶ Third, he teaches that even if they have not embraced the faith of Christ, they are not therefore to be deprived of liberty, possession, and dominion over the things which nature has granted them — indeed, that they may lawfully use and enjoy them.

Fourth, he teaches that they are not to be extirpated from the face of the earth by violence, tyranny, and the atrocity of arms, lest, goaded by these hardships, they vomit forth blasphemies against the law of Christ.

¶ Fifth, he teaches that those who carried out atrocious and savage acts against them in violation of the commands and prohibitions of the Kings of Castile were satellites of Satan and of the most relentless enemy of humankind.

¶ Sixth, he teaches that the western and southern Indians who have not heard the Gospel of Christ are to be invited and led to the faith by gentleness, meekness, kind inducements, and Christian charity.

You see the Supreme Pontiff, regarding the drawing of unbelievers to the faith, decreeing the same thing that natural reason itself dictates, that Christ taught and commanded by word and example, that his apostles, always following his footsteps in all things, took care to fulfill diligently, that the sacred doctors taught in their writings, and that the Church has used through its traditions and customs from the earliest age, and which it shows preachers of the faith ought to employ.

Therefore they are not to be harassed, despoiled, and persecuted — which is what the satellites of Satan have done and continue to do, raging against a wretched people with incredible savagery — nor are they even to be punished for any sins, however grave, in which they may be entangled, committed within the bounds of their own territories or in their unbelief, as the good doctor Sepúlveda rashly and presumptuously affirms.
¶ Seventh, the bull declares and, making a new decree, ordains that acts done contrary to this are void and of no effect.

By these eleven arguments our truthful judgment is proven to be most true and beyond doubt — namely, that it does not belong to the Church, nor to any Christian prince, to punish the crimes of unbelievers of which our discourse treats, by reason of defect of authority and jurisdiction, and so forth.
